title: Heart attack warning signs: Most heart attacks inv...
content: Heart attack warning signs: Most heart attacks involve discomfort in the center of the chest that lasts more than a few minutes, or goes away and comes back. It can feel like uncomfortable pressure, squeezing, fullness, or pain. Symptoms also include pain or discomfort in one or both arms, the back, neck, jaw, or stomach. Shortness of breath may occur with or without chest discomfort. Other signs include breaking out in a cold sweat, nausea, or lightheadedness. If you notice these symptoms, call 911 immediately. Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

title: Fever management in adults: A fever is a temperatu...
content: Fever management in adults: A fever is a temperature above 100.4°F (38°C). Most fevers caused by infections are not dangerous. For adults, a fever under 103°F can generally be managed at home with rest and fluids. Take acetaminophen or ibuprofen to reduce discomfort. Seek medical care if the fever is above 103°F, lasts more than 3 days, or is accompanied by a stiff neck, severe headache, rash, confusion, or difficulty breathing.

title: Fever in children: A temperature above 100.4°F (38...
content: Fever in children: A temperature above 100.4°F (38°C) in infants younger than 3 months requires immediate emergency evaluation. For children 3 months to 3 years, call a doctor for any fever above 102.2°F. Give children's acetaminophen or ibuprofen based on their weight. Never give aspirin to a child. Encourage fluids to prevent dehydration. Watch for signs of serious illness: stiff neck, rash that doesn't fade, difficulty breathing, or persistent vomiting.

title: Minor burns treatment: For first-degree burns (red...
content: Minor burns treatment: For first-degree burns (redness, no blisters) and small second-degree burns, run cool (not ice cold) water over the burn for 10 minutes. Do not use butter, toothpaste, or ice. Cover with a sterile non-stick bandage. Take ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain. Seek emergency care if the burn covers a large area, affects the face, hands, feet, genitals, or a major joint, appears deep or white/charred, or if the person inhaled smoke.

title: Chest pain assessment: Chest pain can have many ca...
content: Chest pain assessment: Chest pain can have many causes. Cardiac causes include heart attack, angina, and pericarditis. Non-cardiac causes include acid reflux (GERD), muscle strain, anxiety, and pneumonia. Red flags that suggest cardiac emergency include: pressure or squeezing feeling in the chest, pain radiating to the arm or jaw, sweating, nausea, and shortness of breath. Sharp localized pain that changes with position is more likely musculoskeletal. When in doubt, call 911.

title: Stroke recognition with FAST: Use the FAST acronym...
content: Stroke recognition with FAST: Use the FAST acronym to identify a stroke. F - Face drooping: Is one side of the face drooping or numb? Ask the person to smile. A - Arm weakness: Is one arm weak or numb? Ask them to raise both arms. S - Speech difficulty: Is their speech slurred or hard to understand? T - Time to call 911: If any of these signs are observed, even if symptoms pass, call 911 immediately. Time is critical — stroke treatments are most effective within the first few hours.

title: Allergic reaction and anaphylaxis: Mild allergic r...
content: Allergic reaction and anaphylaxis: Mild allergic reactions include hives, itching, or a runny nose and can be treated with antihistamines like diphenhydramine. Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening reaction with symptoms including hives, swelling of the throat, difficulty breathing, a drop in blood pressure, and dizziness. If someone has an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen), use it immediately for signs of anaphylaxis, then call 911. Do not wait to see if symptoms improve on their own.

title: Appendicitis symptoms: Appendicitis typically caus...
content: Appendicitis symptoms: Appendicitis typically causes pain that starts around the belly button and moves to the lower right abdomen. The pain usually worsens over hours and is aggravated by movement. Other symptoms include low-grade fever, nausea, vomiting, and loss of appetite. If appendicitis is suspected, seek emergency care immediately. If the appendix ruptures, symptoms may temporarily improve before worsening significantly, which is a medical emergency. Do not take laxatives or use heating pads on the abdomen.

title: Asthma attack management: During an asthma attack,...
content: Asthma attack management: During an asthma attack, have the person sit upright and use their prescribed rescue inhaler (such as albuterol) as directed, typically 2 puffs. Wait 20 minutes. If symptoms do not improve, take 2 more puffs. Seek emergency care if the person's lips or fingernails turn blue, if they are unable to speak in full sentences, if breathing is very labored, or if the rescue inhaler provides no relief after 2 doses. Ensure the person is not lying flat.

title: Diabetic hypoglycemia (low blood sugar): Signs of ...
content: Diabetic hypoglycemia (low blood sugar): Signs of low blood sugar include shakiness, sweating, confusion, rapid heartbeat, and blurry vision. If the person is conscious and able to swallow, give 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrates: 4 glucose tablets, half a cup of juice, or regular (not diet) soda. Recheck blood sugar after 15 minutes and repeat if still low. If the person is unconscious or cannot swallow, call 911 immediately and do not give anything by mouth. Glucagon injection may be needed.

title: Common medications and side effects - Lisinopril: ...
content: Common medications and side effects - Lisinopril: Lisinopril is an ACE inhibitor used to treat high blood pressure and heart failure. Common side effects include a dry, persistent cough (the most frequent reason for stopping the drug), dizziness, headache, and mild fatigue. Serious side effects requiring immediate medical attention include angioedema (swelling of the face, lips, tongue, or throat which can block the airway), a severe drop in blood pressure, and kidney problems indicated by reduced urination.

title: When to go to the emergency room vs urgent care: G...
content: When to go to the emergency room vs urgent care: Go to the ER for: chest pain, difficulty breathing, stroke symptoms, severe bleeding that does not stop, loss of consciousness, major trauma, severe allergic reactions, seizures, and high fever with stiff neck. Urgent care is appropriate for: minor cuts needing stitches, ear infections, urinary tract infections, mild to moderate asthma attacks, sprains, minor burns, vomiting and diarrhea without signs of dehydration, and fevers without serious symptoms.

title: Diabetes: Recognizing Hypoglycemia (Low Blood Sugar)
content: Hypoglycemia occurs when blood sugar levels drop below 70 mg/dL. Symptoms include shakiness, nervousness, sweating, chills, irritability, confusion, rapid heartbeat, and hunger. If conscious, use the '15-15 rule': eat 15 grams of fast-acting carbs (like 4 oz of juice or glucose tablets), wait 15 minutes, and recheck. If blood sugar is still low, repeat. Seek emergency care if the person is unconscious or symptoms are severe.

title: Cold vs. Flu (Influenza)
content: Colds usually develop slowly and include a runny nose, sore throat, and mild cough. Fever is rare. The flu comes on suddenly and is much more severe. Symptoms include high fever (100°F+), intense muscle aches, chills, fatigue, and a dry cough. Seek medical care if you have difficulty breathing, chest pain, or a fever that won't go down with medication.

title: Sprains and Strains: The RICE Method
content: For minor muscle strains or joint sprains, use the RICE method: Rest (avoid using the injured area), Ice (apply cold packs for 20 minutes several times a day), Compression (use an elastic bandage), and Elevation (keep the injured area above heart level). Seek medical care if you cannot bear weight on the joint, the area is numb, or there is obvious deformity.

title: Heat Exhaustion vs. Heatstroke
content: Heat exhaustion symptoms include heavy sweating, rapid pulse, dizziness, fatigue, and nausea. Move to a cool place, drink water, and use cool compresses. Heatstroke is a life-threatening emergency where the body temperature rises above 104°F. Symptoms include confusion, hot/dry skin (no sweating), rapid pulse, and loss of consciousness. Call 911 immediately for suspected heatstroke.
